sections.content:
The Student Management Tool is the project I think about most when someone talks about human-centered design. Not because it went well.

I built a desktop app for my tutoring job without a single stakeholder conversation. Technically it worked. My colleagues were enthusiastic. And then they pointed out features that were, in retrospect, completely obvious. The features they surfaced were exactly what a basic conversation would have caught on day one. Designing without the people you're designing for is just guessing with extra steps.

WordBond started from the opposite premise. The iterative evaluation, pilot study, then formal study with standardised scales, kept surfacing real issues and real improvements. Results showed the emotional dimension of the design, the voice-bond between parent and child, was landing even in a simulated setting. The significant limitation is one I name clearly in the report: we never tested with actual visually impaired parents. Simulating impairment and living with it are not the same thing.

WordBond is also where my vision felt most directly at work, AI serving a human experience rather than replacing it. Keeping a parent's voice in a reading moment that belongs to them, not to a text-to-speech engine. The distance between those two projects is the most honest measure of where my thinking on User and Society shifted.
